artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick update on Eli, Mom, and the garden

Hi Mom,

House is finally quiet – Daniel’s grading at the dining table, Eli’s in bed with his Wisconsin history book, and Nora passed out halfway through her graphic novel. Figured I’d send you an update before I forget everything.

Eli’s been doing better since that awful bullying mess on the Fox Prairie playground last April. He still tenses up about recess, but Ms. Lauren Peters has been great. She and I worked out that he and two kids from her social-emotional skills group can slip over to the library after school some days and work in the “quiet corner” we’re setting up in the Stoughton Youth Community Garden behind the library instead of hanging on the crowded playground. I’m planning one raised bed that’s just theirs so it feels like a calm, safe spot, not punishment.

Money-wise, you know things are still tight with Eli’s $4,800 appendectomy bill from Stoughton Hospital last June. We’ve been on that 24‑month payment plan since August 2023, so I can’t front lumber or soil, but I can pour in time and kid-wrangling. The pilot beds already helped – Ms. Kendra Olson at Fox Prairie said during Eli’s free-and-reduced-lunch review in September 2024 that his veggie intake is up, and honestly our grocery bill at 742 Summit Ridge Drive is down about $40–$50 a month.

How are you feeling after chemo this week at Carbone? I keep thinking about what Dr. Patel said after your July 2023 stroke at St. Mary’s – light outdoor activity and more fresh vegetables – and wishing you lived closer so you could come to our Thursday evening sessions and take home a big bag of greens instead of frozen dinners.

Love you,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Quick update on Eli, Mom, and the garden

artifacts.content:
Subject: Mom helping with the garden + Sofia

Hey Claire,

I’ve been thinking about you a lot, especially with Mom’s March 2024 breast cancer diagnosis at UW Health in Madison and all the trips to the Carbone Cancer Center. The chemo has wiped her out. Between that and her left knee replacement at UW Health back in March 2023, mobility is such an issue. One bright spot: she’s actually excited about our Tuesday afternoon intergenerational gardening sessions behind the Stoughton Public Library. I’m planning to get a couple of good kneeling benches and long-handled tools so she can help the kids without risking a fall when she tries to get down to ground level.

Selfishly, it feels good to have her out there modeling “garden grandparent” energy. Dr. Patel reminded us after her minor stroke and July 2023 discharge from St. Mary’s that light outdoor activity and fresh veggies would help with blood pressure and cholesterol, so the garden gives her that when she visits.

On the teen side, Sofia Martinez (the 16‑year‑old I told you about) has stepped up as a student leader. In early May 2024 she pulled me and Erica Jensen aside in the teen area at the library and admitted she’s been coming to after-school garden planning meetings instead of going straight home because of constant fights about money and her dad’s OWI arrest in Dane County. She said having a calm, structured place to go in Stoughton makes her feel safer. Hearing that, plus what Pastor Greg shared on October 3, 2024 about older adults at the Stoughton United Methodist free community meal sometimes skipping blood pressure meds or insulin to afford food, just reinforces why this little patch of dirt matters.

Anyway, how are you and Mark? Next time you’re in town, bring the kids – I’ll put them to work watering.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Mom helping with the garden + Sofia

artifacts.content:
Title: Garden program brainstorm – teens, SEL, and access

Date: 2025-01-29

- Teen peer leaders: Plan for 3 stipends again. One slot reserved for Daniel’s 8th grader from Stoughton Middle School – the one who’s been on a behavior plan since his suspension in October 2023. Per conversation with school social worker Mr. Kevin Ruiz, structured after-school leadership at the Stoughton Youth Community Garden behind the library could support his plan instead of just more detentions.

- Tuesday evening reflection circle: keep piloting with the three middle schoolers Dr. Karen Olsen at SSM Health Behavioral Health in Madison has been seeing for self-harm thoughts. Since my own intake with her back in June 2022, we’ve talked about how working in the garden and then writing or drawing about feelings could reinforce the coping strategies she’s teaching. Need to use a small portion of funds to buy extra garden journals, markers, colored pencils.

- My own capacity: remember I’m still on a reduced 15 hours/week at the Stoughton Public Library after the panic attack during that crowded teen program in February 2024 and starting with Dr. Karen Ellison at Stoughton Behavioral Health Clinic in January 2024. Outdoor, repetitive tasks are part of my anxiety plan, but I need clear boundaries so I don’t burn out.

- Dignified access: build on Maria Sanchez’s idea from her late August 2024 home visit – garden as a quiet way for middle schoolers on her confidential food-pantry referral list (including one of Daniel’s 7th graders) to pick up produce without classmates knowing. They can simply say they’re “helping with the garden project.”

metadata.title: Garden program brainstorm – teens, SEL, and access

artifacts.content:
Title: Thursday Evening Garden Session w/ Mom + Pantry Drop

Date: 2025-08-14
Time: 5:30–8:00 p.m.
Location: Stoughton Youth Community Garden, behind Stoughton Public Library, 214 Oak Street
Attendees: Melissa, Mom (Janice), Eli, Nora, teen leaders, “garden grandparents”

Agenda/Notes:

- 5:30–6:00 – Set up adaptive tools and seating for Mom. Remember her left knee replacement at UW Health (March 2023) + minor stroke and July 2023 discharge from St. Mary’s in Madison: keep tasks light (herb harvesting, supervising kids at the wash station). Follow Dr. Patel’s advice: gentle outdoor movement + fresh vegetables for BP and cholesterol.

- 6:00–7:00 – Youth work time. Quiet corner bed check-in with Eli and the two kids Ms. Lauren Peters referred from her Fox Prairie social-emotional skills group after the April 2024 bullying incident on the playground. Make sure it still feels like a calm alternative to crowded recess, not “time-out.”

- 7:00–7:30 – Harvest for donations: aim for 10–15 lbs of tomatoes, greens, herbs to set aside for UW Health Carbone Cancer Center oncology pantry (for Mom and other chemo patients) plus a crate for the Stoughton food pantry.

- 7:30–8:00 – Walk-through with teen leaders: include Daniel’s 8th grader on the October 2023 behavior plan and one of Maria Sanchez’s middle schoolers from her confidential food-pantry referral list. Emphasize leadership roles (weighing produce, labeling crates) and confidentiality/dignity for families.

Reminders: Bring Mom’s hat, water bottle, and an extra tote so she can take home produce instead of frozen dinners at the apartment.

metadata.title: Thursday Evening Garden Session w/ Mom + Pantry Drop
metadata.location: Stoughton Public Library garden, 214 Oak Street, Stoughton, WI 53589

artifacts.content:
Subject: Nora, Lucas, and trying to make space feel safe

Hi Alison,

Thanks again for meeting with me about Nora. It’s still hard to believe this all started back around October 2023 with “just” name‑calling on the Fox Prairie playground and then escalated to that shoving incident. I’m still shaken from the night we ended up at UW Health urgent care in Fitchburg in November 2023 to rule out a concussion.

I really appreciate you and the school counselor helping us think through calmer after‑school options. Getting Nora over to the library on garden days has made such a difference – she comes home from the Stoughton Youth Community Garden behind the library more relaxed, especially when she’s helping water the quiet corner beds Eli uses when his anxiety flares.

I also wanted to give you a heads-up that our schedule will sometimes wobble around Daniel’s court‑ordered visitation with his 13‑year‑old, Lucas. Dane County Family Court updated the schedule in October 2023, and on “Lucas weekends” we’re trying to fold his community service hours from the minor vaping citation at Badger Middle School into the library garden workdays. The assistant principal there signed off on counting the hours, and I’m hoping being outside with dirt under his nails will feel more like a fresh start than punishment.

If you hear of any other kids who might benefit from a calm, supervised space after school – especially those dealing with bullying or food insecurity – please feel free to nudge families toward the garden. I’ve already been coordinating with Maria Sanchez on a few of her food‑pantry referral students so they can pick up produce discreetly.

Thank you for everything you’re doing for these kids.

Warmly,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Nora, Lucas, and trying to make space feel safe

artifacts.content:
Thread with: “Jess – church & soccer”
Date: 2025-06-02

Melissa: Hey, are you still on for helping chop veggies at the Stoughton United Methodist free meal this Thursday?

Jess: Yep! Do I need to bring anything?

Melissa: Just yourself. Pastor Greg is hoping for more tomatoes + greens from the library beds. When I met with him back on October 3, 2024, he told me some of the older folks from the low‑income apartments on East Main Street admitted they sometimes skip blood pressure meds or insulin because they can’t afford both prescriptions and fresh food. Hearing that made me double‑down on getting the garden going.

Jess: That’s heartbreaking. I’m glad you’re doing this.

Melissa: Same. It’s all connected. Between Mom’s chemo at Carbone since her March 2024 breast cancer diagnosis and Eli’s free‑and‑reduced‑lunch review with Ms. Kendra Olson last September, I keep seeing how much food + health overlap. Kendra actually suggested Eli join the first youth group in the garden so he’d have more consistent access to fresh produce. Our grocery bill at 742 Summit Ridge has dropped like $40–$50 a month since he started bringing home tomatoes and greens from the pilot beds.

Jess: That’s huge.

Melissa: It helps, especially with that $4,800 bill from his emergency appendectomy at Stoughton Hospital last June. We’ve been on a 24‑month payment plan since August 2023, so I’m mostly donating time, not money. But watching the kids pick cucumbers and then serve them at church or send them home with Maria Sanchez’s pantry kids? Worth every sweaty hour.

Jess: Okay, now I’m definitely coming Thursday. 😊

Melissa: Yay. I’ll save you a cutting board.

Title: Personal reflection – why this garden matters (even on hard days)

Date: 2025-03-15
Location: Living room couch, 742 Summit Ridge

Some days I wonder if I’ve taken on too much. Since January 2024, when I started seeing Dr. Karen Ellison at Stoughton Behavioral Health Clinic after that awful panic attack during the crowded teen program at the library in February, I’ve been on reduced hours (15/week) and trying to be more honest about my anxiety and depression. In session on 3/12/24 we talked about how being outside, doing repetitive physical tasks – weeding, watering, hauling mulch – actually calms my nervous system in a way the fluorescent teen room never will.

The garden has become part of my treatment plan, but it’s also become this web that connects so many stories: Eli’s anxiety flare after the April 2024 bullying incident on the Fox Prairie playground and his quiet corner bed with the two kids from Ms. Peters’ social-emotional group; Nora’s shove and the November 2023 urgent care trip to UW Health Fitchburg, and how different she is on days she comes straight to the beds instead of the playground.

I think about the three middle schoolers Dr. Olsen and I talked about at SSM Health Behavioral Health in Madison – the Tuesday evening reflection circle kids who are wrestling with self-harm thoughts and use their garden journals to draw out the feelings. I think about Maria Sanchez’s pantry list, Daniel’s 8th grader on the October 2023 behavior plan, Sofia escaping her dad’s OWI chaos, Mom’s chemo and stroke recovery.

It’s a lot. But when I’m out there, hands in the soil behind the Stoughton Public Library, it feels like all of us are breathing a little easier together.

metadata.title: Personal reflection – why this garden matters (even on hard days)
